AI.tldr: The article provides a six-step decision framework for organizations adopting Large Language Models (LLMs) to overcome challenges related to security, infrastructure, and deployment, with real-world examples from healthcare, finance, and software industries.
AI.motivation: Organizations are increasingly adopting LLMs but lack clear guidance on implementation and adoption decisions, despite their potential in various domains.
AI.method: The framework is developed based on extensive interviews and analysis of successful and failed LLM implementations, providing a systematic approach for LLM adoption.
AI.result: The framework offers practical guidance through six key decision points and real-world examples from B2B and B2C contexts, helping organizations align LLM capabilities with business objectives.
AI.conclusion: The six-step framework enables organizations to make informed LLM adoption decisions, ensuring secure and efficient integration across use cases such as customer service, content creation, and analytics.

title: Towards Harnessing the Power of LLMs for ABAC Policy Mining
summary: This paper presents an empirical investigation into the capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) to perform automated Attribute-based Access Control (ABAC) policy mining. While ABAC provides fine-grained, context-aware access management, the increasing number and complexity of access policies can make their formulation and evaluation rather challenging. To address the task of synthesizing concise yet accurate policies, we evaluate the performance of some of the state-of-the-art LLMs, specifically Google Gemini (Flash and Pro) and OpenAI ChatGPT, as potential policy mining engines. An experimental framework was developed in Python to generate randomized access data parameterized by varying numbers of subjects, objects, and initial policy sets. The baseline policy sets, which govern permission decisions between subjects and objects, serve as the ground truth for comparison. Each LLM-generated policy was evaluated against the baseline policy using standard performance metrics. The results indicate that LLMs can effectively infer compact and valid ABAC policies for small-scale scenarios. However, as the system size increases, characterized by higher numbers of subjects and objects, LLM outputs exhibit declining accuracy and precision, coupled with significant increase in the size of policy generated, which is beyond the optimal size. These findings highlight both the promise and limitations of current LLM architectures for scalable policy mining in access control domains. Future work will explore hybrid approaches that combine prompt optimization with classical rule mining algorithms to improve scalability and interpretability in complex ABAC environments.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates the effectiveness of LLMs in mining ABAC policies, finding they work for small systems but struggle with scalability.
AI.motivation: ABAC policy formulation and evaluation is challenging due to the increasing number and complexity of access policies, prompting the investigation into LLMs as a policy mining solution.
AI.method: An experimental framework was developed to generate access data and evaluate LLMs on policy mining performance, comparing generated policies to baseline sets using metrics.
AI.result: LLMs infer valid ABAC policies for small-scale scenarios but show reduced accuracy and precision with larger systems, generating overly complex policies.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs have promise for policy mining but face scalability issues, suggesting future work with hybrid approaches for improved performance.

title: From Reviewers' Lens: Understanding Bug Bounty Report Invalid Reasons with LLMs
comment: 10 pages, 4 figures
summary:
Bug bounty platforms (e.g., HackerOne, BugCrowd) leverage crowd-sourced vulnerability discovery to improve continuous coverage, reduce the cost of discovery, and serve as an integral complement to internal red teams. With the rise of AI-generated bug reports, little work exists to help bug hunters understand why these reports are labeled as invalid. To improve report quality and reduce reviewers' burden, it is critical to predict invalid reports and interpret invalid reasons.
  In this work, we conduct an empirical study with the purpose of helping bug hunters understand the validity of reports. We collect a dataset of 9,942 disclosed bug bounty reports, including 1,400 invalid reports, and evaluate whether state-of-the-art large language models can identify invalid reports. While models such as GPT-5, DeepSeek, and a fine-tuned RoBERTa achieve strong overall accuracy, they consistently struggle to detect invalid cases, showing a tendency to over-accept reports. To improve invalidity detection, we build a taxonomy of rejection reasons for Information Disclosure vulnerabilities and incorporate it into a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework. This approach substantially improves classification consistency and reduces bias. We also examine whether reviewer decisions may be influenced by factors beyond the content of the report. Our analysis shows that reporters with higher reputations tend to receive more favorable outcomes in borderline cases, suggesting that perceived expertise can influence review judgments.
  Overall, our findings highlight the challenges of invalid report identification and show that combining LLMs with structured reviewer knowledge can support more transparent and consistent vulnerability report review.

AI.tldr: Researchers analyze invalid bug bounty report detection using LLMs and find that while models struggle with accuracy, combining them with a rejection taxonomy improves consistency. However, reviewer bias toward high-reputation reporters persists.
AI.motivation: The motivation stems from the need to address the lack of tools for identifying invalid AI-generated bug reports, which hampers report quality and reviewer efficiency. Accurate invalidity detection is critical to reduce workload and ensure fair evaluation.
AI.method: The authors collected a dataset of 9,942 public bug bounty reports, evaluated state-of-the-art LLMs (GPT-5, DeepSeek, fine-tuned RoBERTa) for invalid report detection, developed a taxonomy of rejection reasons for Information Disclosure vulnerabilities, and incorporated it into a RAG framework to improve classification consistency and reduce bias. They also analyzed factors influencing reviewer decisions beyond report content.
AI.result: While LLMs achieved strong overall accuracy, they struggled to detect invalid reports and exhibited over-acceptance bias. The RAG-based taxonomy improved classification consistency but revealed that higher-reputation reporters receive more favorable outcomes in borderline cases, indicating that perceived expertise influences reviewer judgments.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that integrating large language models with structured reviewer knowledge enhances the transparency and consistency of vulnerability report reviews, particularly in identifying invalid reports and mitigating biases related to reporter reputation.

title: ASTRA: Agentic Steerability and Risk Assessment Framework
summary:
Securing AI agents powered by Large Language Models (LLMs) represents one of the most critical challenges in AI security today. Unlike traditional software, AI agents leverage LLMs as their "brain" to autonomously perform actions via connected tools. This capability introduces significant risks that go far beyond those of harmful text presented in a chatbot that was the main application of LLMs. A compromised AI agent can deliberately abuse powerful tools to perform malicious actions, in many cases irreversible, and limited solely by the guardrails on the tools themselves and the LLM ability to enforce them. This paper presents ASTRA, a first-of-its-kind framework designed to evaluate the effectiveness of LLMs in supporting the creation of secure agents that enforce custom guardrails defined at the system-prompt level (e.g., "Do not send an email out of the company domain," or "Never extend the robotic arm in more than 2 meters").
  Our holistic framework simulates 10 diverse autonomous agents varying between a coding assistant and a delivery drone equipped with 37 unique tools. We test these agents against a suite of novel attacks developed specifically for agentic threats, inspired by the OWASP Top 10 but adapted to challenge the ability of the LLM for policy enforcement during multi-turn planning and execution of strict tool activation. By evaluating 13 open-source, tool-calling LLMs, we uncovered surprising and significant differences in their ability to remain secure and keep operating within their boundaries. The purpose of this work is to provide the community with a robust and unified methodology to build and validate better LLMs, ultimately pushing for more secure and reliable agentic AI systems.

AI.tldr: ASTRA reveals critical security gaps in autonomous LLM agents by testing policy enforcement across diverse tasks and tools, demonstrating wide disparities in LLM performance for secure system design.
AI.motivation: Current LLM security measures are insufficient for autonomous agents that perform irreversible actions via external tools, necessitating new evaluation methods to enforce system-prompt guardrails in multi-step operations.
AI.method: The authors developed ASTRA, a framework testing 10 autonomous agents with 37 tools against tailored agentic threats adapted from OWASP Top 10. This involves multi-turn policy enforcement evaluation across 13 open-source LLMs.
AI.result: Evaluation revealed significant and surprising variation among LLMs in maintaining security boundaries during tool activation, highlighting weaknesses in existing systems.
AI.conclusion: ASTRA provides a foundational framework for evaluating and improving the security of LLM-powered AI agents, establishing a benchmark for developing robust agentic systems with enforceable guardrails.

title: Leveraging Discrete Choice Experiments for User-Centric Requirements Prioritization in mHealth Applications
summary: Mobile health (mHealth) applications are widely used for chronic disease management, but usability and accessibility challenges persist due to the diverse needs of users. Adaptive User Interfaces (AUIs) offer a personalized solution to enhance user experience, yet barriers to adoption remain. Understanding user preferences and trade-offs is essential to ensure widespread acceptance of adaptation designs. This study identifies key factors influencing user preferences and trade-offs in mHealth adaptation design. A Discrete Choice Experiment (DCE) was conducted with 186 participants who have chronic diseases and use mHealth applications. Participants were asked to select preferred adaptation designs from choices featuring six attributes with varying levels. A mixed logit model was used to analyze preference heterogeneity and determine the factors most likely influencing adoption. Additionally, subgroup analyses were performed to explore differences by age, gender, health conditions, and coping mechanisms. Maintaining usability while ensuring controllability over adaptations, infrequent adaptations, and small-scale changes are key factors that facilitate the adoption of adaptive mHealth app designs. In contrast, frequently used functions and caregiver involvement can diminish the perceived value of such adaptations. This study employs a data-driven approach to quantify user preferences, identify key trade-offs, and reveal variations across demographic and behavioral subgroups through preference heterogeneity modeling. Furthermore, our results offer valuable guidance for developing future adaptive mHealth applications and lay the groundwork for continued exploration into requirements prioritization within the field of software engineering.

AI.tldr: This DCE study with 186 mHealth users identifies usability, control, and adaptation frequency as critical factors influencing adaptive mHealth app design adoption, while revealing subgroup differences that inform personalized software development strategies.
AI.motivation: Despite AUIs' potential to improve mHealth accessibility, adoption barriers persist. This study addresses the need to systematically understand user preferences and trade-offs to optimize adaptation designs for diverse populations.
AI.method: A Discrete Choice Experiment (DCE) with 186 chronic disease patients was conducted, using a mixed logit model to analyze preference heterogeneity across six adaptive design attributes. Subgroup analyses (age, gender, health conditions) explored adoption factors.
AI.result: Key adoption enablers: usability + controllability, infrequent adaptations, small-scale changes. Barriers include caregiver involvement and high adaptation frequency. Subgroup analyses confirmed significant preference variations across demographics/health profiles.
AI.conclusion: This study provides data-driven guidance for designing adaptive mHealth applications by quantifying user preferences, identifying critical trade-offs, and uncovering demographic/behavioral subgroup differences. It establishes a foundation for future software engineering priorities in mHealth design.

title: eBPF-PATROL: Protective Agent for Threat Recognition and Overreach Limitation using eBPF in Containerized and Virtualized Environments
summary: With the increasing use and adoption of cloud and cloud-native computing, the underlying technologies (i.e., containerization and virtualization) have become foundational. However, strict isolation and maintaining runtime security in these environments has become increasingly challenging. Existing approaches like seccomp and Mandatory Access Control (MAC) frameworks offer some protection up to a limit, but often lack context awareness, syscall argument filtering, and adaptive enforcement, providing the ability to adjust decisions at runtime based on observed application behavior, workload changes, or detected anomalies rather than relying solely on static or predefined rules.This paper introduces eBPF-PATROL (eBPF-Protective Agent for Threat Recognition and Overreach Limitation), an extensible lightweight runtime security agent that uses extended Berkeley Packet Filter (eBPF) technology to monitor and enforce policies in containerized and virtualized environments. By intercepting system calls, analyzing execution context, and applying user-defined rules, eBPF-PATROL detects and prevents real-time boundary violations, such as reverse shells, privilege escalation, and container escape attempts. We describe the architecture, implementation, and evaluation of eBPF-PATROL, demonstrating its low overhead (< 2.5 percent) and high detection accuracy across real-world attack scenarios.

AI.tldr: eBPF-PATROL is a lightweight runtime security solution using eBPF to dynamically detect & prevent attacks in cloud environments (like containers). It achieves <2.5% overhead with high accuracy against threats like reverse shells and container escapes.
AI.motivation: The paper highlights the increasing challenges in securing cloud-native environments (containers/virtualization) due to rigid isolation requirements and shortcomings in current security solutions. Existing tools like seccomp and MAC frameworks lack context awareness, adaptive enforcement, and dynamic adjustment capabilities.
AI.method: The method involves designing eBPF-PATROL to leverage eBPF technology for intercepting system calls and analyzing execution context in real-time. User-defined rules are applied to detect and prevent boundary violations such as reverse shells, privilege escalation, and container escapes.
AI.result: Empirical results demonstrate less than 2.5% performance overhead and high detection accuracy across real-world attack scenarios. The evaluation confirms eBPF-PATROL's effectiveness in identifying and mitigating security threats in runtime environments.
AI.conclusion: This paper introduces eBPF-PATROL, an innovative runtime security agent that effectively addresses the limitations of existing approaches by utilizing eBPF technology. Its lightweight design and runtime adaptability make it well-suited for protecting cloud-native environments, achieving high detection accuracy with minimal performance overhead.

title: ChroniUXMag: A Persona-Driven Framework for Inclusive mHealth Requirements Engineering
summary: Mobile health (mHealth) applications are increasingly adopted for chronic disease management, yet they face persistent challenges related to accessibility, inclusivity, and sustained engagement. Patients' needs evolve dynamically with their health progression, adherence, and caregiver support, creating unique requirements engineering (RE) challenges that traditional approaches often overlook. This study introduces ChroniUXMag, a framework for eliciting and analysing inclusivity requirements in mHealth design. Building on InclusiveMag and GenderMag principles, the framework aims to help researchers and practitioners systematically capture and evaluate factors that influence how individuals with chronic conditions perceive, trust, and interact with mHealth systems. The framework was developed through two stages of the InclusiveMag process. In the first stage, inclusivity facets were identified through a systematic literature review, focus groups, interviews, and a large-scale survey. In the second stage, these facets were synthesised into personas representing diverse health situations, attitudes, and digital practices, and integrated into an adapted cognitive walkthrough form. Thirteen facets were identified that capture the socio-technical complexity of mHealth use, including trust, digital literacy, dependency, and cultural context. These facets support structured, persona-driven evaluations that reveal inclusivity barriers often missed by traditional usability assessments. ChroniUXMag contributes to RE by offering a reproducible, evidence-based approach for embedding inclusivity into mHealth requirements. Future work will extend the third stage Apply through practitioner-led evaluation in real-world design contexts.

AI.tldr: ChroniUXMag is a new framework for mHealth design that captures inclusivity requirements through personas and 13 key facets, improving accessibility for chronic disease patients by addressing evolving needs traditional methods overlook.
AI.motivation: Traditional requirements engineering fails to address dynamic needs of chronic disease patients regarding accessibility, trust, and engagement in mHealth adoption, creating unique inclusivity challenges.
AI.method: Built on InclusiveMag/GenderMag principles, the framework was developed via two stages: (1) literature review, surveys, focus groups, and interviews to identify 13 inclusivity facets (e.g., trust, digital literacy), and (2) synthesis into personas and an adapted cognitive walkthrough form for evaluating mHealth design.
AI.result: Structured evaluations reveal 13 socio-technical facets influencing mHealth interactions, enabling identification of inclusivity barriers missed by conventional usability assessments. Framework supports persona-driven analysis of trust, dependency, cultural context, and digital literacy.
AI.conclusion: ChroniUXMag provides a reproducible, evidence-based framework for embedding inclusivity into mHealth requirements, addressing gaps in traditional approaches through structured evaluations of inclusivity barriers.

title: A Novel and Practical Universal Adversarial Perturbations against Deep Reinforcement Learning based Intrusion Detection Systems
comment: 13 pages, 7 Figures,
summary: Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) play a vital role in defending modern cyber physical systems against increasingly sophisticated cyber threats. Deep Reinforcement Learning-based IDS, have shown promise due to their adaptive and generalization capabilities. However, recent studies reveal their vulnerability to adversarial attacks, including Universal Adversarial Perturbations (UAPs), which can deceive models with a single, input-agnostic perturbation. In this work, we propose a novel UAP attack against Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL)-based IDS under the domain-specific constraints derived from network data rules and feature relationships. To the best of our knowledge, there is no existing study that has explored UAP generation for the DRL-based IDS. In addition, this is the first work that focuses on developing a UAP against a DRL-based IDS under realistic domain constraints based on not only the basic domain rules but also mathematical relations between the features. Furthermore, we enhance the evasion performance of the proposed UAP, by introducing a customized loss function based on the Pearson Correlation Coefficient, and we denote it as Customized UAP. To the best of our knowledge, this is also the first work using the PCC value in the UAP generation, even in the broader context. Four additional established UAP baselines are implemented for a comprehensive comparison. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed Customized UAP outperforms two input-dependent attacks including Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM), Basic Iterative Method (BIM), and four UAP baselines, highlighting its effectiveness for real-world adversarial scenarios.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel Universal Adversarial Perturbation (UAP) attack against Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL)-based Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) under domain-specific constraints, enhanced with a Pearson Correlation Coefficient-based loss function, which outperforms existing attacks in experiments.
AI.motivation: To address the vulnerability of DRL-based IDS to UAP attacks and develop a more effective attack method under realistic domain constraints.
AI.method: Propose a UAP attack for DRL-based IDS by incorporating domain-specific constraints derived from network data rules and feature relationships, and introduce a Customized UAP using a loss function based on Pearson Correlation Coefficient.
AI.result: The proposed Customized UAP outperforms existing UAP baselines and input-dependent attacks (FGSM, BIM) in experimental results.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the proposed Customized UAP is effective against DRL-based IDS under domain constraints and demonstrates superior performance in real-world adversarial scenarios.

title: Summary-Mediated Repair: Can LLMs use code summarisation as a tool for program repair?
comment: 6 pages, 3 tables, 1 figure
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) often produce code with subtle implementation-level bugs despite strong benchmark performance. These errors are hard for LLMs to spot and can have large behavioural effects; yet when asked to summarise code, LLMs can frequently surface high-level intent and sometimes overlook this low-level noise. Motivated by this, we propose summary-mediated repair, a prompt-only pipeline for program repair that leverages natural-language code summarisation as an explicit intermediate step, extending previous work that has already shown code summarisation to be a useful intermediary for downstream tasks. We evaluate our method across eight production-grade LLMs on two function level benchmarks (HumanEvalPack and MBPP), comparing several summary styles against a direct repair baseline. Error-aware diagnostic summaries consistently yield the largest gains - repairing up to 65% of unseen errors, on average of 5% more than the baseline - though overall improvements are modest and LLM-dependent. Our results position summaries as a cheap, human-interpretable diagnostic artefact that can be integrated into program-repair pipelines rather than a stand-alone fix-all.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces summary-mediated repair, a new prompt-only method for fixing program errors by using code summaries as an intermediate step. It shows that using error-aware diagnostic summaries can fix more errors compared to direct repair methods, but the improvements vary between models.
AI.motivation: Despite strong performance on benchmarks, Large Language Models (LLMs) often produce code with subtle bugs that are hard for them to identify. Code summarization, however, often captures high-level intent and overlooks low-level errors, making it a potential tool for detecting and fixing those errors.
AI.method: The method involves creating code summaries for problematic code and using those as an intermediate step to diagnose and repair errors. Several summary styles are evaluated against a direct repair baseline across multiple LLMs and two function-level benchmarks.
AI.result: Error-aware diagnostic summaries significantly improved program repair success, with up to 65% of unseen errors being repaired, an average improvement of 5% over the baseline. The gains were consistent across models but varied in effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Summary-mediated repair demonstrates that summaries can be a cost-effective and human-interpretable diagnostic tool within program-repair pipelines, though it is not a complete replacement for direct repair techniques.

title: Utilizing Circulant Structure to Optimize the Implementations of Linear Layers
summary: In this paper, we propose a novel approach for optimizing the linear layer used in symmetric cryptography. It is observed that these matrices often have circulant structure. The basic idea of this work is to utilize the property to construct a sequence of transformation matrices, which allows subsequent heuristic algorithms to find more efficient implementations. Our results outperform previous works for various linear layers of block ciphers. For Whirlwind M0 , we obtain two implementations with 159 XOR counts (8% better than Yuan et al. at FSE 2025) and depth 17 (39% better than Shi et al. at AsiaCrypt 2024) respectively. For AES MixColumn, our automated method produces a quantum circuit with depth 10, which nearly matches the manually optimized state-of-the-art result by Zhang et al. at IEEE TC 2024, only with 2 extra CNOTs.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel optimization method for the linear layers in symmetric cryptography, leveraging the circulant structure of these matrices. The proposed technique outperforms existing works for specific implementations, such as Whirlwind M0 and AES MixColumn.
AI.motivation: Linear layers in symmetric cryptography often use circulant matrices, which can be optimized for more efficient implementation.
AI.method: The paper constructs a sequence of transformation matrices based on the circulant structure, enabling heuristic algorithms to find more efficient implementations.
AI.result: For Whirlwind M0, two implementations are achieved with 159 XOR counts (8% improvement) and depth 17 (39% improvement). For AES MixColumn, a quantum circuit with depth 10 is produced, matching state-of-the-art results with only 2 extra CNOTs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively optimizes linear layers in symmetric cryptography, achieving better performance compared to existing approaches.

title: Optimizing LLM Code Suggestions: Feedback-Driven Timing with Lightweight State Bounds
comment: \c{opyright} 2025 IEEE. Personal use of this material is permitted. Permission from IEEE must be obtained for all other uses
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have transformed code auto-completion by generating context-aware suggestions. Yet, deciding when to present these suggestions remains underexplored, often leading to interruptions or wasted inference calls. We propose an adaptive timing mechanism that dynamically adjusts the delay before offering a suggestion based on real-time developer feedback. Our suggested method combines a logistic transform of recent acceptance rates with a bounded delay range, anchored by a high-level binary prediction of the developer's cognitive state. In a two-month deployment with professional developers, our system improved suggestion acceptance from 4.9% with no delay to 15.4% with static delays, and to 18.6% with adaptive timing-while reducing blind rejections (rejections without being read) from 8.3% to 0.36%. Together, these improvements increase acceptance and substantially reduce wasted inference calls by 75%, making LLM-based code assistants more efficient and cost-effective in practice.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an adaptive timing mechanism for code auto-completion with Large Language Models, enhancing suggestion acceptance and reducing wasted calls in professional settings
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the gap in research regarding when to present code auto-completion suggestions generated by Large Language Models, aiming to reduce interruptions and wasted inference calls.
AI.method: The method combines a logistic transform of recent acceptance rates with a bounded delay range and a binary prediction of the developer's cognitive state to dynamically adjust the suggestion delay.
AI.result: The system achieved an 18.6% suggestion acceptance rate with adaptive timing, a significant improvement over 4.9% with no delay and 15.4% with static delays, while also reducing blind rejections by 75%.
AI.conclusion: The adaptive timing mechanism effectively improves the efficiency and cost-effectiveness of LLM-based code assistants by optimizing the timing of suggestion delivery through real-time developer feedback.

title: Think Fast: Real-Time IoT Intrusion Reasoning Using IDS and LLMs at the Edge Gateway
summary: As the number of connected IoT devices continues to grow, securing these systems against cyber threats remains a major challenge, especially in environments with limited computational and energy resources. This paper presents an edge-centric Intrusion Detection System (IDS) framework that integrates lightweight machine learning (ML) based IDS models with pre-trained large language models (LLMs) to improve detection accuracy, semantic interpretability, and operational efficiency at the network edge. The system evaluates six ML-based IDS models: Decision Tree (DT), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Random Forest (RF), Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), and a hybrid CNN-LSTM model on low-power edge gateways, achieving accuracy up to 98 percent under real-world cyberattacks. For anomaly detection, the system transmits a compact and secure telemetry snapshot (for example, CPU usage, memory usage, latency, and energy consumption) via low-bandwidth API calls to LLMs including GPT-4-turbo, DeepSeek V2, and LLaMA 3.5. These models use zero-shot, few-shot, and chain-of-thought reasoning to produce human-readable threat analyses and actionable mitigation recommendations. Evaluations across diverse attacks such as DoS, DDoS, brute force, and port scanning show that the system enhances interpretability while maintaining low latency (<1.5 s), minimal bandwidth usage (<1.2 kB per prompt), and energy efficiency (<75 J), demonstrating its practicality and scalability as an IDS solution for edge gateways.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an edge-centric IDS framework combining lightweight machine learning models and pre-trained large language models to enhance detection accuracy and interpretability while maintaining operational efficiency for resource-constrained IoT devices.
AI.motivation: Securing resource-limited IoT environments against cyber threats requires solutions that balance high detection accuracy with computational/energy efficiency.
AI.method: Integrated six ML-based IDS models (DT, KNN, RF, CNN, LSTM, CNN-LSTM) on edge gateways with LLMs (GPT-4-turbo, DeepSeek V2, LLaMA 3.5) for anomaly analysis using compressed telemetry data transmitted via low-bandwidth API calls.
AI.result: Achieved 98% detection accuracy; demonstrated low latency (<1.5s), minimal bandwidth usage (<1.2kB prompt), and energy efficiency (<75J) across DoS, DDoS, brute force, and port scanning attacks.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid framework successfully balances accuracy, interpretability, and resource efficiency, offering a practical and scalable IDS solution for edge gateways in IoT systems.

title: Pre-Filtering Code Suggestions using Developer Behavioral Telemetry to Optimize LLM-Assisted Programming
comment: \c{opyright} 2025 IEEE. Personal use of this material is permitted. Permission from IEEE must be obtained for all other uses
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly integrated into code editors to provide AI-powered code suggestions. Yet many of these suggestions are ignored, resulting in wasted computation, increased latency, and unnecessary interruptions. We introduce a lightweight pre-filtering model that predicts the likelihood of suggestion acceptance before invoking the LLM, using only real-time developer telemetry such as typing speed, file navigation, and editing activity. Deployed in a production-grade Visual Studio Code plugin over four months of naturalistic use, our approach nearly doubled acceptance rates (18.4% -> 34.2%) while suppressing 35% of low-value LLM calls. These findings demonstrate that behavioral signals alone can meaningfully improve both user experience and system efficiency in LLM-assisted programming, highlighting the value of timing-aware, privacy-preserving adaptation mechanisms. The filter operates solely on pre-invocation editor telemetry and never inspects code or prompts.

AI.tldr: Real-time behavioral signals (e.g., typing, navigation) predict code suggestion acceptance before LLM activation in an IDE plugin. This method boosted acceptance rates by 82% and reduced 35% of low-value calls without inspecting code or prompts.
AI.motivation: Many AI code suggestions are ignored, leading to wasted computation, increased latency, and unnecessary interruptions. The paper addresses the inefficiency of LLM-assisted tools by improving acceptance rates while reducing low-value LLM calls.
AI.method: A lightweight pre-filtering model was developed, utilizing real-time developer telemetry such as typing speed, file navigation, and editing activity. This model predicts the likelihood of suggestion acceptance before triggering the LLM, operating solely on pre-invocation data and without inspecting code or prompts.
AI.result: The approach achieved a 1.86× increase in acceptance rates (18.4% → 34.2%) and suppressed 35% of low-value LLM calls in a production Visual Studio Code plugin. This demonstrates improved system efficiency without compromising user experience.
AI.conclusion: Behavioral signals can significantly enhance user experience and system efficiency in LLM-assisted programming. The study highlights the importance of timing-aware and privacy-preserving adaptation mechanisms.

title: Lightweight Autoencoder-Isolation Forest Anomaly Detection for Green IoT Edge Gateways
summary: The rapid growth of the Internet of Things (IoT) has given rise to highly diverse and interconnected ecosystems that are increasingly susceptible to sophisticated cyber threats. Conventional anomaly detection schemes often prioritize accuracy while overlooking computational efficiency and environmental impact, which limits their deployment in resource-constrained edge environments. This paper presents \textit{EcoDefender}, a sustainable hybrid anomaly detection framework that integrates \textit{Autoencoder(AE)}-based representation learning with \textit{Isolation Forest(IF)} anomaly scoring. Beyond empirical performance, EcoDefender is supported by a theoretical foundation that establishes formal guarantees for its stability, convergence, robustness, and energy-complexity coupling-thereby linking computational behavior to energy efficiency. Furthermore, experiments on realistic IoT traffic confirm these theoretical insights, achieving up to 94\% detection accuracy with an average CPU usage of only 22\%, 27 ms inference latency, and 30\% lower energy consumption compared to AE-only baselines. By embedding sustainability metrics directly into the security evaluation process, this work demonstrates that reliable anomaly detection and environmental responsibility can coexist within next-generation green IoT infrastructures, aligning with the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDG 9: resilient infrastructure, SDG 13: climate action).

AI.tldr: EcoDefender is a sustainable hybrid anomaly detection framework that combines AE and IF for IoT security, offering high accuracy, low resource usage, and energy efficiency.
AI.motivation: The growth of IoT has led to complex ecosystems vulnerable to cyber threats. Traditional methods lack computational efficiency and environmental impact awareness, making them unsuitable for resource-constrained edge environments.
AI.method: EcoDefender integrates Autoencoder-based representation learning with Isolation Forest anomaly scoring. It includes theoretical analysis of stability, convergence, robustness, and energy-complexity coupling.
AI.result: Experiments on IoT traffic show 94\% detection accuracy with low CPU usage (22\%), latency (27 ms), and 30\% lower energy consumption compared to AE baselines.
AI.conclusion: EcoDefender demonstrates that effective anomaly detection can be both reliable and environmentally sustainable, supporting SDG 9 and SDG 13 by providing a framework for green IoT infrastructures.

title: Time Travel: LLM-Assisted Semantic Behavior Localization with Git Bisect
comment: submitted to Git Bisect SCALCOM 2025 Calgary (to be published)
summary: We present a novel framework that integrates Large Language Models (LLMs) into the Git bisect process for semantic fault localization. Traditional bisect assumes deterministic predicates and binary failure states assumptions often violated in modern software development due to flaky tests, nonmonotonic regressions, and semantic divergence from upstream repositories. Our system augments bisect traversal with structured chain of thought reasoning, enabling commit by commit analysis under noisy conditions. We evaluate multiple open source and proprietary LLMs for their suitability and fine tune DeepSeekCoderV2 using QLoRA on a curated dataset of semantically labeled diffs. We adopt a weak supervision workflow to reduce annotation overhead, incorporating human in the loop corrections and self consistency filtering. Experiments across multiple open source projects show a 6.4 point absolute gain in success rate from 74.2 to 80.6 percent, leading to significantly fewer failed traversals and by experiment up to 2x reduction in average bisect time. We conclude with discussions on temporal reasoning, prompt design, and finetuning strategies tailored for commit level behavior analysis.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a framework integrating LLMs into Git bisect for semantic fault localization, addressing challenges from flaky tests and nonmonotonic regressions. Experiments show a 6.4% improvement in success rate.
AI.motivation: Traditional Git bisect methods assume deterministic predicates and binary failure states, which are often violated in modern software development due to flaky tests, nonmonotonic regressions, and semantic divergence. These issues lead to inaccurate fault localization and inefficient debugging processes.
AI.method: The proposed system enhances Git bisect by incorporating structured chain of thought reasoning, allowing for commit-by-commit analysis under noisy conditions. The authors evaluated various LLMs, fine-tuned DeepSeekCoderV2 using QLoRA on a semantically labeled diff dataset, and employed a weak supervision workflow to minimize annotation effort.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple open-source projects demonstrated a 6.4 percentage point increase in success rate, from 74.2% to 80.6%, with up to a 2x reduction in average bisect time. The system also reduced failed traversals significantly.
AI.conclusion: The integration of LLMs into Git bisect improves fault localization accuracy and efficiency, especially in the presence of noisy and non-deterministic conditions. The paper discusses temporal reasoning, prompt design, and fine-tuning strategies for commit-level behavior analysis, highlighting opportunities for further research in this area.

title: Carbon-Aware Intrusion Detection: A Comparative Study of Supervised and Unsupervised DRL for Sustainable IoT Edge Gateways
summary: The rapid expansion of the Internet of Things (IoT) has intensified cybersecurity challenges, particularly in mitigating Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) attacks at the network edge. Traditional Intrusion Detection Systems (IDSs) face significant limitations, including poor adaptability to evolving and zero-day attacks, reliance on static signatures and labeled datasets, and inefficiency on resource-constrained edge gateways. Moreover, most existing DRL-based IDS studies overlook sustainability factors such as energy efficiency and carbon impact. To address these challenges, this paper proposes two novel Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL)-based IDS: DeepEdgeIDS, an unsupervised Autoencoder-DRL hybrid, and AutoDRL-IDS, a supervised LSTM-DRL model. Both DRL-based IDS are validated through theoretical analysis and experimental evaluation on edge gateways. Results demonstrate that AutoDRL-IDS achieves 94% detection accuracy using labeled data, while DeepEdgeIDS attains 98% accuracy and adaptability without labels. Distinctly, this study introduces a carbon-aware, multi-objective reward function optimized for sustainable and real-time IDS operations in dynamic IoT networks.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes two sustainable DRL-based IDS for IoT edge security: DeepEdgeIDS (98% accuracy, no labels) and AutoDRL-IDS (94% accuracy), with a novel carbon-aware reward function for real-time, zero-day attack detection.
AI.motivation: Existing IDS struggle with evolving/zero-day IoT threats at the network edge due to three limitations: (1) poor adaptability to new attacks, (2) dependence on static signatures and labeled datasets, and (3) inefficiency on resource-constrained devices. Additionally, prior DRL approaches ignore sustainability (energy/carbon impact) in IDS design.
AI.method: The paper introduces two original DRL-based IDS architectures: (1) DeepEdgeIDS, an unsupervised Autoencoder-DRL hybrid for zero-day attack detection without labeled data, and (2) AutoDRL-IDS, a supervised LSTM-DRL model using temporal traffic patterns. Both models are validated with theoretical analysis and edge gateway experiments.
AI.result: Empirical results demonstrate 94% detection accuracy for AutoDRL-IDS with labeled data, and 98% accuracy with anomaly detection capabilities for DeepEdgeIDS in unsupervised scenarios. The carbon-aware multi-objective reward function enables sustainable real-time DDoS mitigation on edge gateways.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the proposed DRL-based IDS models improve detection accuracy, adaptability to evolving threats, and sustainability in dynamic IoT edge environments, particularly through the novel carbon-aware reward function.

title: VecIntrinBench: Benchmarking Cross-Architecture Intrinsic Code Migration for RISC-V Vector
comment: 5 pages, 7 figures
summary: Intrinsic functions are specialized functions provided by the compiler that efficiently operate on architecture-specific hardware, allowing programmers to write optimized code in a high-level language that fully exploits hardware features. Using intrinsics to vectorize core code blocks is a standard optimization method in high-performance libraries, often requiring specific vector optimization implementations for multiple mainstream architectures. The promising RISC-V software ecosystem has a significant demand for algorithm library migration and adaptation. Translating existing intrinsic functions to RISC-V Vector (RVV) intrinsic functions across architectures is currently a mainstream approach. Rule-based intrinsic mapping methods and LLM-based code generation can help developers address the code migration challenge. However, existing intrinsic code benchmarks focus on mainstream SIMD intrinsics and lack support for the emerging RISC-V architecture. There is currently no benchmark that comprehensively evaluates the intrinsic migration capabilities for the RVV extension. To fill this gap, we propose VecIntrinBench, the first intrinsic benchmark encompassing RVV extensions. It includes 50 function-level tasks from open source repositories, implemented as scalars, RVV intrinsics, Arm Neon intrinsics, and x86 intrinsics, along with comprehensive functional and performance test cases. We systematically evaluated various code migration approaches on VecIntrinBench, yielding a series of insightful findings. The results demonstrate that advanced Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve a similar effect as rule-based mapping approaches for RISC-V code migration, while also delivering superior performance. We further analyze the reasons and identify future directions for LLM development in the code migration field. The VecIntrinBench is open-sourced to benefit the broader community and developers.

AI.tldr: This paper creates VecIntrinBench to evaluate RISC-V Vector intrinsic migration, proving LLMs outperform rule-based methods in code adaptation while being made open-source.
AI.motivation: RISC-V's growing ecosystem demands algorithm library adaptation, but lacks benchmarks for RVV intrinsic migration, hindering cross-architecture optimization and LLM-based code generation evaluation.
AI.method: Developed VecIntrinBench, a benchmark with 50 function-level tasks across scalar, RVV, Arm Neon, and x86 intrinsics, validated with functional/performance tests and evaluated rule-based methods and LLM-based code generation.
AI.result: Results show LLMs match rule-based mapping efficacy for RISC-V code migration while delivering superior performance, with identified optimization patterns driving future LLM development directions for code migration.
AI.conclusion: VecIntrinBench is proposed as a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating RISC-V Vector (RVV) intrinsic migration, demonstrating that advanced LLMs achieve comparable or superior performance to rule-based approaches in code migration, offering insights for future development and open-sourced to benefit the community.

title: On Addressing Isolation in Blockchain-Based Self-Sovereign Identity
summary: Self-Sovereign Identity (SSI) grants holders full ownership and control of their digital identities, being the ultimate digital identity model. Operating in a decentralized manner, SSI enables the verification of claims, including privacy-preserving mechanisms. Blockchain, which can be used to implement a Verifiable Data Registry (VDR), is often considered one of the pillars of SSI, along with Decentralized Identifiers (DIDs) and Verifiable Credentials (VCs). Unfortunately, blockchains are mostly siloed, affecting the interoperability and universality of SSI. We investigate the effect of blockchain isolation on blockchain-based SSI. We first define possible scenarios for cross-chain SSI and exemplify with real-life use cases. We then define specific requirements for cross-chain SSI and identify challenges, also in relation to the identified scenarios. We explore various solutions to achieve blockchain interoperability, with a focus on SSI. In particular, we identify the advantages and disadvantages of distinct cross-chain models for cross-chain SSI. Finally, we address the usability of cross-chain SSI and discuss security and privacy aspects, opening the way for future research.

AI.tldr: This paper examines blockchain isolation's impact on SSI, proposes cross-chain scenarios, and evaluates interoperability solutions for decentralized identity systems.
AI.motivation: Blockchain isolation limits the interoperability and universality of Self-Sovereign Identity (SSI). The paper investigates how this isolation affects SSI and seeks solutions to enable seamless cross-chain operations.
AI.method: The authors define cross-chain SSI scenarios with real-world use cases, establish requirements and challenges, explore interoperability solutions, compare cross-chain models, and analyze usability, security, and privacy aspects.
AI.result: The paper identifies feasible cross-chain scenarios, challenges, and solutions for blockchain interoperability in SSI. It provides a structured analysis of models, their trade-offs, and outlines directions for future research.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that achieving interoperability in blockchain-based Self-Sovereign Identity (SSI) requires addressing cross-chain challenges through specific models and solutions. It emphasizes the need for further research to enhance usability, security, and privacy in cross-chain SSI systems.

title: Optimization-Aware Test Generation for Deep Learning Compilers
comment: This paper has been accpected by ICSE 2026
summary: Deep Learning (DL) compilers have been widely utilized to optimize DL models for efficient deployment across various hardware. Due to their vital role in the DL ecosystem, ensuring their reliability and security is critical. However, existing approaches have limitations in testing optimization stages, which is the core functionality of DL compilers, due to the difficulty in generating optimization-aware tests. In this paper, we proposed OATest, a novel approach for synthesizing optimization-aware computational graphs. The approach combines patterns extracted from documented tests for optimization and incorporates them into seed computational graphs, enabling broader exploration of optimization paths. To guarantee the optimization-awareness of generated graphs, OATest introduces the edges reusing strategy to establish strong connections between patterns and contexts. Additionally, to solve the validity challenge for the generated graphs, OATest employs an auxiliary layers addition strategy to resolve broken constraints. Equipped with two distinct test oracles, OATest applies differential testing to evaluate the two widely used DL compilers (i.e., TVM and ONNXRuntime). Our experimental results show that OATest outperforms the state-of-the-art method by detecting more bugs and achieving higher code coverage in TVM and ONNXRutimes. Additionally, OATest uncovers 58 previously unknown bugs, 36 of which have been confirmed or fixed by developers.

AI.tldr: OATest is a novel testing approach for deep learning compilers that generates optimization-aware computational graphs to find more bugs and achieve higher coverage compared to existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing methods lack effective testing of DL compiler optimization stages due to difficulties in generating relevant tests. This limits reliability assurances for these critical components.
AI.method: OATest synthesizes optimization-aware computational graphs by integrating patterns from existing tests into seed graphs, employing edge reusing strategies for contextual connections and auxiliary layers to resolve validity issues. It uses differential testing with two test oracles to evaluate compilers like TVM and ONNXRuntime.
AI.result: OATest outperforms existing methods by detecting more bugs and achieving higher code coverage in TVM and ONNXRuntime. It uncovered 58 previously unknown bugs, with 36 confirmed/fixed by developers.
AI.conclusion: OATest proves effective in enhancing the testing of deep learning compilers by enabling more thorough optimization-aware computational graph generation, leading to the discovery of numerous new bugs and higher code coverage.

title: ioPUF+: A PUF Based on I/O Pull-Up/Down Resistors for Secret Key Generation in IoT Nodes
summary: In this work, we present ioPUF+, which incorporates a novel Physical Unclonable Function (PUF) that generates unique fingerprints for Integrated Circuits (ICs) and the IoT nodes encompassing them. The proposed PUF generates device-specific responses by measuring the pull-up and pull-down resistor values on the I/O pins of the ICs, which naturally vary across chips due to manufacturing-induced process variations. Since these resistors are already integrated into the I/O structures of most ICs, ioPUF+ requires no custom circuitry, and no new IC fabrication. This makes ioPUF+ suitable for cost-sensitive embedded systems built from Commercial Off-The-Shelf (COTS) components. Beyond introducing a new PUF, ioPUF+ includes a complete datapath for converting raw PUF responses into cryptographically usable secret keys using BCH error correction and SHA-256 hashing. Further ioPUF+ also demonstrate a practical use case of PUF derive secret keys in securing device-to-device communication using AES-encryption. We implemented ioPUF+ on the Infineon PSoC-5 microcontroller and evaluated its performance across 30 devices using standard PUF metrics. The results show excellent reliability (intra-device Hamming distance of 100.00%), strong uniqueness (inter-device Hamming distance of 50.33%), near-ideal uniformity (50.54%), and negligible bit aliasing. Stability tests under temperature and supply-voltage variations show worst-case bit-error rates of only 2.63% and 2.10%, respectively. We also profiled the resource and energy usage of the complete ioPUF+ system, including the PUF primitive, BCH decoding, SHA-256 hashing, and AES encryption. The full implementation requires only 19.8 KB of Flash, exhibits a latency of 600 ms, and consumes 79 mW of power, demonstrating the suitabilitiy of ioPUF+ for resource-constrained IoT nodes.

AI.tldr: ioPUF+ is a new PUF that generates unique IC fingerprints by measuring resistor values on I/O pins without custom circuitry, and it includes a secure key derivation process with low resource requirements suitable for IoT devices.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind this paper is to create a low-cost and practical Physical Unclonable Function (PUF) for securing Integrated Circuits (ICs) and IoT nodes, using existing IC components to avoid the need for custom fabrication.
AI.method: The method uses naturally varying pull-up and pull-down resistor values on I/O pins of ICs to generate device-specific PUF responses. The raw PUF outputs are processed through BCH error correction and SHA-256 hashing to generate cryptographic keys, and AES encryption is used to demonstrate a practical use case.
AI.result: The paper shows results with 100% intra-device reliability, 50.33% inter-device uniqueness, 50.54% uniformity, and minimal bit aliasing. Stability under temperature and voltage variations has bit error rates of 2.63% and 2.10%, respectively. The system requires 19.8 KB Flash, 600 ms latency, and 79 mW power.
AI.conclusion: ioPUF+ is a promising secure solution for resource-limited IoT environments, as it leverages existing IC components and provides strong cryptographic key generation capabilities with low overhead and high robustness.

title: LLM-Driven Kernel Evolution: Automating Driver Updates in Linux
summary: Linux kernel evolution breaks drivers through API/ABI changes, semantic shifts, and security-hardening updates. We introduce DRIVEBENCH, an executable corpus of kernel$\rightarrow$driver co-evolution cases, and AUTODRIVER, a closed-loop, LLM-driven system for automating driver maintenance. The system integrates prompt engineering, multi-agent collaboration, static analysis, and iterative validation to ensure that generated patches are not only syntactically correct but also functionally and semantically consistent with kernel conventions. The corpus spans v5.10-v6.10 with 235 validated cases drawn from 612 candidates. In evaluation across 55 cases, AUTODRIVER achieves 56.4% compilation success; QEMU-based boot verification indicates that compiled patches preserve driver initialization in most instances. By releasing DRIVEBENCH and tooling, we enable reproducible research and a practical route to continuous, safe co-evolution of drivers with the Linux kernel.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes DRIVEBENCH (a kernel-driver co-evolution corpus) and AUTODRIVER (an LLM-powered driver maintenance system), demonstrating compilation success on kernel v5.10-v6.10 driver updates.
AI.motivation: Linux kernel updates frequently break drivers through API/ABI changes, semantic shifts, and security hardening, creating a need for automated maintenance solutions.
AI.method: The system combines prompt engineering, multi-agent collaboration, static analysis, and iterative validation to generate syntactically and semantically correct driver patches.
AI.result: AUTODRIVER achieves 56.4% compilation success on 55 test cases, with QEMU verification showing most patches preserve driver initialization.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that releasing DRIVEBENCH and AUTODRIVER enables reproducible research and a practical solution for continuous, safe kernel-driver co-evolution.

title: LLMs as Firmware Experts: A Runtime-Grown Tree-of-Agents Framework
comment: 18 pages, 13 figures
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) and their agent systems have recently demonstrated strong potential in automating code reasoning and vulnerability detection. However, when applied to large-scale firmware, their performance degrades due to the binary nature of firmware, complex dependency structures, and heterogeneous components. To address this challenge, this paper presents FIRMHIVE, a recursive agent hive that enables LLMs to act as autonomous firmware security analysts. FIRMHIVE introduces two key mechanisms: (1) transforming delegation into a per-agent, executable primitive and (2) constructing a runtime Tree of Agents (ToA) for decentralized coordination. We evaluate FIRMHIVE using real-world firmware images obtained from publicly available datasets, covering five representative security analysis tasks. Compared with existing LLM-agent baselines, FIRMHIVE performs deeper (about 16x more reasoning steps) and broader (about 2.3x more files inspected) cross-file exploration, resulting in about 5.6x more alerts per firmware. Compared to state-of-the-art (SOTA) security tools, FIRMHIVE identifies about 1.5x more vulnerabilities (1,802 total) and achieves 71% precision, representing significant improvements in both yield and fidelity.

AI.tldr: FIRMHIVE introduces a recursive agent hive with decentralized task delegation and runtime coordination, achieving state-of-the-art firmware vulnerability detection with significantly deeper/broader code analysis compared to existing LLM-agent and security tools.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-agent systems struggle with firmware security due to binaries' complexity, dependency structures, and heterogeneous components, leading to performance degradation in large-scale analysis.
AI.method: The framework employs two core mechanisms: (1) transforming delegation into a per-agent executable primitive for task distribution and (2) a runtime Tree of Agents (ToA) structure enabling decentralized coordination across firmware components.
AI.result: Evaluations show 16× deeper reasoning steps, 2.3× more files inspected, 5.6× more alerts per firmware, and 1.5× more vulnerabilities detected than LLM baselines, plus 71% precision identifying 1,802 total vulnerabilities exceeding SOTA tools.
AI.conclusion: FIRMHIVE significantly enhances firmware security analysis via LLM agents by introducing execution delegation primitives and decentralized agent coordination, outperforming existing methods and tools in depth, breadth, and precision.

title: LLMAID: Identifying AI Capabilities in Android Apps with LLMs
summary:
Recent advancements in artificial intelligence (AI) and its widespread integration into mobile software applications have received significant attention, highlighting the growing prominence of AI capabilities in modern software systems. However, the inherent hallucination and reliability issues of AI continue to raise persistent concerns. Consequently, application users and regulators increasingly ask critical questions such as: Does the application incorporate AI capabilities? and What specific types of AI functionalities are embedded? Preliminary efforts have been made to identify AI capabilities in mobile software; however, existing approaches mainly rely on manual inspection and rule-based heuristics. These methods are not only costly and time-consuming but also struggle to adapt advanced AI techniques.
  To address the limitations of existing methods, we propose LLMAID (Large Language Model for AI Discovery). LLMAID includes four main tasks: (1) candidate extraction, (2) knowledge base interaction, (3) AI capability analysis and detection, and (4) AI service summarization. We apply LLMAID to a dataset of 4,201 Android applications and demonstrate that it identifies 242% more real-world AI apps than state-of-the-art rule-based approaches. Our experiments show that LLM4AID achieves high precision and recall, both exceeding 90%, in detecting AI-related components. Additionally, a user study indicates that developers find the AI service summaries generated by LLMAID to be more informative and preferable to the original app descriptions. Finally, we leverage LLMAID to perform an empirical analysis of AI capabilities across Android apps. The results reveal a strong concentration of AI functionality in computer vision (54.80%), with object detection emerging as the most common task (25.19%).

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LLMAID, an innovative framework utilizing a Large Language Model for the automated detection of AI capabilities in mobile applications. Through four distinct tasks, it surpasses current rule-based methods by identifying significantly more AI applications with high precision and recall. Empirical analysis highlights a significant focus on computer vision, especially object detection, within Android apps.
AI.motivation: The increasing use of AI in mobile applications is accompanied by challenges in reliability and hallucination. Current identification methods for AI capabilities are manual or rule-based, leading to inefficiencies and lack of adaptability to advanced AI techniques.  The need for a more effective and efficient method for identifying AI capabilities is crucial for users, developers and regulators.
AI.method: The proposed  solution, LLMAID (Large Language Model for AI Discovery), is built on a four-task framework: (1) candidate extraction, (2) knowledge base interaction, (3) AI capability analysis and detection, and (4) AI service summarization. This framework leverages the power of large language models to overcome the limitations of existing approaches.
AI.result: LLMAID was applied to a dataset of 4,201 Android applications. Results show significant improvement over state-of-the-art rule-based methods with a 242% increase in identifying AI applications, while maintaining high precision and recall ( both over 90%).  A user study further demonstrates the superiority of LLMAID-generated AI service summaries over original app descriptions.  The framework was also used to conduct an empirical analysis of AI capabilities in Android apps, revealing a strong emphasis on computer vision (54.80%), with object detection being the most prevalent task (25.19%).
AI.conclusion: LLMAID provides an efficient and accurate solution for detecting AI capabilities in mobile applications. By outperforming existing methods, the framework offers a more effective means of addressing the challenges posed by the increasing use of AI in software systems.  The insights gained from the empirical analysis further underscore the prevalent use of computer vision tasks, particularly object detection, in Android applications.

title: Shadows in the Code: Exploring the Risks and Defenses of LLM-based Multi-Agent Software Development Systems
comment: Accepted by AAAI 2026 Alignment Track
summary: The rapid advancement of Large Language Model (LLM)-driven multi-agent systems has significantly streamlined software developing tasks, enabling users with little technical expertise to develop executable applications. While these systems democratize software creation through natural language requirements, they introduce significant security risks that remain largely unexplored. We identify two risky scenarios: Malicious User with Benign Agents (MU-BA) and Benign User with Malicious Agents (BU-MA). We introduce the Implicit Malicious Behavior Injection Attack (IMBIA), demonstrating how multi-agent systems can be manipulated to generate software with concealed malicious capabilities beneath seemingly benign applications, and propose Adv-IMBIA as a defense mechanism. Evaluations across ChatDev, MetaGPT, and AgentVerse frameworks reveal varying vulnerability patterns, with IMBIA achieving attack success rates of 93%, 45%, and 71% in MU-BA scenarios, and 71%, 84%, and 45% in BU-MA scenarios. Our defense mechanism reduced attack success rates significantly, particularly in the MU-BA scenario. Further analysis reveals that compromised agents in the coding and testing phases pose significantly greater security risks, while also identifying critical agents that require protection against malicious user exploitation. Our findings highlight the urgent need for robust security measures in multi-agent software development systems and provide practical guidelines for implementing targeted, resource-efficient defensive strategies.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates security risks in multi-agent systems for software development, introducing IMBIA attacks and a defense mechanism (Adv-IMBIA) with varying success rates across frameworks.
AI.motivation: The study is motivated by the lack of security analysis in LLM-driven multi-agent systems, which are becoming increasingly prevalent for software development tasks by non-technical users.
AI.method: The researchers propose the IMBIA attack method to exploit vulnerabilities during software generation and introduce Adv-IMBIA as a defense strategy, evaluating their approach across three major multi-agent frameworks.
AI.result: IMBIA achieved high attack success rates (93%, 45%, and 71% in MU-BA and 71%, 84%, and 45% in BU-MA scenarios). Adv-IMBIA significantly reduced these risks, especially in MU-BA scenarios, with vulnerabilities concentrated in coding and testing phases.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes with a call for stronger security measures in multi-agent software development systems and provides practical defensive strategies based on critical agent identification.

title: Can LLMs Recover Program Semantics? A Systematic Evaluation with Symbolic Execution
summary: Obfuscation poses a persistent challenge for software engineering tasks such as program comprehension, maintenance, testing, and vulnerability detection. While compiler optimizations and third-party code often introduce transformations that obscure program intent, existing analysis tools and large language models (LLMs) struggle to recover the original semantics. In this work, we investigate whether LLMs, when fine-tuned with symbolic execution artifacts, can effectively deobfuscate programs and restore analyzability. We construct a benchmark by applying four widely studied transformations-control-flow flattening, opaque predicates, arithmetic encoding, and branch encoding-across diverse C programs from TUM Obfuscation Benchmarks, the LLVM test suite, and algorithmic repositories. We then compare three state-of-the-art LLMs under two training configurations: baseline fine-tuning on obfuscated/original code pairs, and enhanced fine-tuning with additional KLEE artifacts such as SMT constraints, path statistics, and test cases. Our evaluation examines syntactic correctness (compilation success), semantic fidelity (behavioral equivalence under symbolic execution), and code quality (readability and structure). Results show that GPT-4.1-mini achieves the strongest deobfuscation overall, and that incorporating KLEE artifacts consistently improves semantic preservation and compilation success across models. These findings highlight deobfuscation as a broader software engineering concern, demonstrating that combining LLMs with symbolic execution can strengthen automated testing, static analysis, and program comprehension in the presence of obfuscation.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that fine-tuning LLMs with symbolic execution artifacts (like KLEE) improves deobfuscation. GPT-4.1-mini performed best, with KLEE-enhanced training consistently boosting code recovery across models.
AI.motivation: Obfuscation hinders critical software engineering tasks like maintenance and vulnerability detection. Existing tools and even advanced LLMs fail to recover obfuscated code's original semantics, necessitating improved deobfuscation approaches.
AI.method: The authors constructed a benchmark by applying four obfuscation techniques (control-flow flattening, opaque predicates, arithmetic/branch encoding) to C programs. They evaluated three LLMs under two training setups: baseline fine-tuning on obfuscated/original code pairs, and enhanced fine-tuning with KLEE artifacts (SMT constraints, path statistics, test cases). Evaluation metrics included compilation success, semantic equivalence via symbolic execution, and code quality.
AI.result: GPT-4.1-mini achieved the highest deobfuscation performance overall. Incorporating KLEE artifacts consistently improved semantic fidelity and compilation success across all models tested, demonstrating the value of symbolic execution data in enhancing LLM-based deobfuscation.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights that integrating LLMs with symbolic execution artifacts improves deobfuscation effectiveness, addressing key software engineering challenges. It underscores the potential of this combination to enhance program analysis tasks despite obfuscation.

title: DEXO: A Secure and Fair Exchange Mechanism for Decentralized IoT Data Markets
comment: This is the accepted version of an article published at the IEEE Internet of Things Journal
summary: Opening up data produced by the Internet of Things (IoT) and mobile devices for public utilization can maximize their economic value. Challenges remain in the trustworthiness of the data sources and the security of the trading process, particularly when there is no trust between the data providers and consumers. In this paper, we propose DEXO, a decentralized data exchange mechanism that facilitates secure and fair data exchange between data consumers and distributed IoT/mobile data providers at scale, allowing the consumer to verify the data generation process and the providers to be compensated for providing authentic data, with correctness guarantees from the exchange platform. To realize this, DEXO extends the decentralized oracle network model that has been successful in the blockchain applications domain to incorporate novel hardware-cryptographic co-design that harmonizes trusted execution environment, secret sharing, and smart contract-assisted fair exchange. For the first time, DEXO ensures end-to-end data confidentiality, source verifiability, and fairness of the exchange process with strong resilience against participant collusion. We implemented a prototype of the DEXO system to demonstrate feasibility. The evaluation shows a moderate deployment cost and significantly improved blockchain operation efficiency compared to a popular data exchange mechanism.

AI.tldr: Error
AI.motivation: Error
AI.method: Error
AI.result: Error
AI.conclusion: Error

title: LLMs-Powered Real-Time Fault Injection: An Approach Toward Intelligent Fault Test Cases Generation
summary: A well-known testing method for the safety evaluation and real-time validation of automotive software systems (ASSs) is Fault Injection (FI). In accordance with the ISO 26262 standard, the faults are introduced artificially for the purpose of analyzing the safety properties and verifying the safety mechanisms during the development phase. However, the current FI method and tools have a significant limitation in that they require manual identification of FI attributes, including fault type, location and time. The more complex the system, the more expensive, time-consuming and labour-intensive the process. To address the aforementioned challenge, a novel Large Language Models (LLMs)-assisted fault test cases (TCs) generation approach for utilization during real-time FI tests is proposed in this paper. To this end, considering the representativeness and coverage criteria, the applicability of various LLMs to create fault TCs from the functional safety requirements (FSRs) has been investigated. Through the validation results of LLMs, the superiority of the proposed approach utilizing gpt-4o in comparison to other state-of-the-art models has been demonstrated. Specifically, the proposed approach exhibits high performance in terms of FSRs classification and fault TCs generation with F1-score of 88% and 97.5%, respectively. To illustrate the proposed approach, the generated fault TCs were executed in real time on a hardware-in-the-loop system, where a high-fidelity automotive system model served as a case study. This novel approach offers a means of optimizing the real-time testing process, thereby reducing costs while simultaneously enhancing the safety properties of complex safety-critical ASSs.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an LLM-assisted approach for generating fault test cases for real-time automotive software systems, enhancing the efficiency and effectiveness of fault injection (FI) testing.
AI.motivation: Traditional FI methods are manual, expensive, and inefficient for complex automotive systems, particularly due to the need for identifying fault attributes (type, location, time) and ensuring adequate coverage and representation.
AI.method: The authors investigate the applicability of various LLMs for automatically generating fault test cases from functional safety requirements (FSRs), focusing on classification and generation accuracy. The proposed method uses gpt-4o as the primary model for real-time FI execution on a hardware-in-the-loop setup.
AI.result: The approach achieved an F1-score of 88% for FSR classification and 97.5% for fault TC generation, demonstrating its effectiveness over existing state-of-the-art models. Real-time execution of TCs on a high-fidelity automotive model showed the practical viability of this method.
AI.conclusion: The LLM-assisted fault test case generation method optimizes the real-time testing of complex automotive systems by improving representativeness, coverage criteria, and test efficiency, while also reducing costs and enhancing safety.

title: LockForge: Automating Paper-to-Code for Logic Locking with Multi-Agent Reasoning LLMs
summary: Despite rapid progress in logic locking (LL), reproducibility remains a challenge as codes are rarely made public. We present LockForge, a first-of-its-kind, multi-agent large language model (LLM) framework that turns LL descriptions in papers into executable and tested code. LockForge provides a carefully crafted pipeline realizing forethought, implementation, iterative refinement, and a multi-stage validation, all to systematically bridge the gap between prose and practice for complex LL schemes. For validation, we devise (i) an LLM-as-Judge stage with a scoring system considering behavioral checks, conceptual mechanisms, structural elements, and reproducibility on benchmarks, and (ii) an independent LLM-as-Examiner stage for ground-truth assessment. We apply LockForge to 10 seminal LL schemes, many of which lack reference implementations. Our evaluation on multiple SOTA LLMs, including ablation studies, reveals the significant complexity of the task. We show that an advanced reasoning model and a sophisticated, multi-stage framework like LockForge are required. We release all implementations and benchmarks, providing a reproducible and fair foundation for evaluation of further LL research.

AI.tldr: LockForge is a first-of-its-kind framework using multi-agent LLMs to convert logic locking paper descriptions into executable code and test them, addressing the reproducibility issue in the field.
AI.motivation: Logic locking research faces reproducibility challenges due to lack of open-source code implementations. The authors motivated by the need for systematic, reliable translation between paper descriptions and functional code for LL schemes.
AI.method: LockForge utilizes a multi-agent LLM framework with a four-stage pipeline: forethought, implementation, iterative refinement, and multi-stage validation. Validation involves an LLM-as-Judge stage with a scoring system and LLM-as-Examiner for ground-truth assessment. The framework is applied to 10 LL schemes with no existing reference implementations.
AI.result: LockForge successfully generates and validates code for 10 complex LL schemes. Evaluation across multiple SOTA LLMs, including ablation studies, shows the need for advanced reasoning models and a sophisticated framework like LockForge. All implementations and benchmarks are made publicly available.
AI.conclusion: The framework addresses key challenges in logic locking reproducibility by enabling conversion of paper-based descriptions to verifiable, executable code, setting a foundation for future research. The release of code and benchmarks promotes reproducibility and fair evaluation in the field.

title: Synthesizing Test Cases for Narrowing Specification Candidates
summary: This paper proposes a technique to help choose the best formal specification candidate among a set of alternatives. Given a set of specifications, our technique generates a suite of test cases that, once classified by the user as desirable or not, narrows down the set of candidates to at most one specification. Two alternative solver-based algorithms are proposed, one that generates a minimal test suite, and another that does not ensure minimality. Both algorithms were implemented in a prototype that can be used generate test suites to help choose among alternative Alloy specifications. Our evaluation of this prototype against a large set of problems showed that the optimal algorithm is efficient enough for many practical problems, and that the non-optimal algorithm can scale up to dozens of candidate specifications while still generating reasonably sized test suites.

AI.tldr: A technique is proposed to select the best formal specification from multiple candidates by generating test cases, with two solver-based algorithms implemented in a prototype showing efficiency in practical scenarios.
AI.motivation: The need for selecting the best formal specification from multiple candidates leads to the development of a technique that utilizes test cases for filtering, addressing the challenge of specification selection in formal methods.
AI.method: The method involves generating a test suite through two solver-based algorithms: one ensures minimal test suite while the other does not, both helping to narrow down specifications based on user classification of test cases as desirable or not.
AI.result: Results indicate that the optimal algorithm is efficient for many practical problems, and the non-optimal algorithm scales well, managing dozens of candidates while generating reasonably sized test suites.
AI.conclusion: The proposed technique effectively assists in selecting the best specification using test suites, with the implemented algorithms demonstrating practical efficiency and scalability in evaluations.

title: Zero-Trust Strategies for O-RAN Cellular Networks: Principles, Challenges and Research Directions
summary: Cellular networks have become foundational to modern communication, supporting a broad range of applications, from civilian use to enterprise systems and military tactical networks.
